title: About the Glastonbury Garden Club
content: The Glastonbury Garden Club was founded in 1947 and has met continuously since then, making it one of the oldest garden clubs in Hartford County. We are a volunteer organization open to all Glastonbury residents and garden enthusiasts from neighboring towns. Our mission is to share knowledge, encourage sustainable gardening practices, support local green spaces, and enjoy the company of fellow plant lovers. Meetings are held on the third Tuesday of each month at the Glastonbury Public Library or the Community Center. Annual events include the Spring Seed Swap, the Summer Garden Walk, and the Fall Plant Swap. Membership is $20 per year.

title: Contact
content: To reach the Glastonbury Garden Club, email our president Ruth Callahan at ruth.callahan.ct@gmail.com or call 860-555-0340. For questions about the website, member submissions, or technical matters, contact James Whitfield at james.whitfield.ct@gmail.com. Meeting venue inquiries can be directed to Michael Egan at the Glastonbury Community Center: megan@glastonburycommunity.org or 860-555-0380. We do not have a permanent office; all correspondence is handled by volunteers.

title: Privacy Policy
content: The Glastonbury Garden Club website collects minimal information. If you contact us by email, your address is used only to respond to your inquiry and is not shared with third parties. Member email addresses on the internal list are used exclusively for club communications. We do not use tracking cookies, run advertising, or collect payment information through this site. Membership dues are collected in person at meetings. This policy was last reviewed in January 2026.

title: Members
content: Glastonbury Garden Club current membership roster for 2026. President: Ruth Callahan. Editor and Web Content: Jenny Wilkins. Secretary: Margaret Hooper. Treasurer: Dorothy Walsh. Members at large: Linda Chu, Audrey Simmons, Peggy Doyle, Elena Park, Susan Marsh, and others. Full member directory is available to dues-paying members via the email list. To join, attend any meeting and pay the $20 annual dues. We welcome gardeners of all experience levels, from first-time apartment balcony growers to Master Gardeners.

title: Upcoming Events
content: Upcoming Glastonbury Garden Club events for 2026. June 16, 2026 — Monthly meeting, 7 PM, Glastonbury Public Library. July 15, 2026 — Summer Social and community garden tour, 5 PM, Community Center. August 18, 2026 — Monthly meeting with guest speaker on late-season perennials, 7 PM, Library. September 15, 2026 — Fall planning meeting and seed share, 7 PM, Library. October 17, 2026 — Annual Fall Plant Swap, 10 AM–12 PM, Glastonbury Community Center (main hall). October 20, 2026 — Monthly meeting and plant swap debrief. November 17, 2026 — Holiday decoration workshop, 6 PM, Community Center. title: Newsletter Archive
content: Access archived Glastonbury Garden Club newsletters here. Newsletters are published monthly and cover meeting recaps, member garden spotlights, seasonal planting tips, and club announcements. Archives go back to 2018. To submit content for the newsletter, email Jenny Wilkins at jenny.wilkins@Finthesiss.ai by the 20th of each month. Back issues before 2018 are available in print at the Welles-Turner Memorial Library local history collection.

title: Fall Plant Swap
content: The Glastonbury Garden Club Annual Fall Plant Swap is our most popular event. It is held each October at the Glastonbury Community Center. Participants bring divisions, cuttings, seedlings, and surplus container plants to trade with fellow members and guests. Plants must be clearly labeled with the common name, cultivar if known, and sun or shade requirements. The swap is free and open to the public — you do not need to be a club member to attend, and you do not need to bring as many plants as you take home. Proceeds from the optional bake sale table support the club's scholarship fund.

title: Summer Garden Walk
content: Each June or July the club hosts a self-guided garden walk through the yards of three to five volunteer member hosts. This year's walk featured Dorothy Walsh's ornamental grass collection, Linda Chu's pollinator border, and a visit to the Glastonbury Community Garden plots. Future hosts are always welcome — gardens do not need to be perfect or large. Contact Ruth Callahan if you would like to open your garden for a future walk.

title: Membership Information
content: Annual membership in the Glastonbury Garden Club is $20, payable at any monthly meeting or by mail to our treasurer. Members receive the monthly newsletter, access to the member-only plant library (seed packets and reference books), discounts at Glastonbury Garden Center through our partnership with Tom Braddock, and invitations to all events including the Fall Plant Swap and Spring Seed Swap. Membership runs January through December. New members joining after September pay half-price for the remainder of the year.

title: Club History
content: The Glastonbury Garden Club was organized in the autumn of 1947 by a group of twelve home gardeners who met in the parlor of the old Griswold house on Hebron Avenue. The founding president was Edna Griswold, who served for twelve years. Early club activities focused on civic beautification: the club planted the window boxes at Town Hall and maintained a demonstration garden at the library for many years. In the 1980s the focus shifted toward education and member gardens. The annual plant swap began in 1991. Current membership stands at approximately 40 active members.

title: Resources for New Gardeners
content: Welcome to perennial gardening in Zone 6b Connecticut. Here are resources the club recommends for newcomers. Books: The Well-Tended Perennial Garden by Tracy DiSabato-Aust (the definitive guide to cutting back and deadheading), Hostas: The Complete Guide by Diana Grenfell, and Connecticut Gardener's Companion for local timing. Online: the Connecticut Cooperative Extension has an excellent plant database. The Welles-Turner Memorial Library in Glastonbury carries many gardening titles and subscribes to several horticultural magazines. Come to a meeting and ask questions — our members are happy to share knowledge in person.

title: Hosta Display Garden
content: The Glastonbury Garden Club maintains a small hosta display garden adjacent to the Community Center's north entrance. The planting was established in 2019 and now contains more than 30 named cultivars, with labels. It is open to the public at all times. Maintenance is done by club volunteers — Jenny Wilkins leads the spring cleanup crew in April each year. If you would like to add a division of a named cultivar to the display, contact Jenny at jenny.wilkins@Finthesiss.ai.

title: Speakers and Workshops
content: The Glastonbury Garden Club hosts a guest speaker or workshop approximately six times per year. Past topics have included integrated pest management, rain garden design, composting, and an introduction to botanical watercolor. Upcoming: August 2026 meeting will feature a regional Master Gardener speaking on late-season perennials. To propose a program or recommend a speaker, contact Ruth Callahan. We pay a modest honorarium to outside speakers and welcome proposals from club members.

title: Spring Seed Swap
content: Each April the club holds a spring seed swap at the monthly meeting. Members bring excess seed packets — commercial or saved — to share. Seeds are labeled and laid out on the library table; members take what interests them and leave what they have extra. There is no requirement to bring seeds to take some. Popular items include annual cosmos, zinnias, marigolds, and saved hosta seeds. Saved perennial seeds from named cultivars may not breed true, but they always grow something interesting. Jenny Wilkins typically brings saved echinacea and rudbeckia.

title: Newsletter Archive: 2025
content: Complete archive of Glastonbury Garden Club newsletters for 2025. January through December issues are available as PDF downloads below. Highlights from 2025: the fall plant swap drew 34 attendees; Jenny Wilkins began the hosta cultivar series in the May newsletter; the club sponsored two library programs on native plants; Dorothy Walsh and Linda Chu were featured in member spotlights. Newsletter editor for 2025 was Margaret Hooper.

title: Newsletter Archive: 2024
content: Complete archive of Glastonbury Garden Club newsletters for 2024. The 2024 program year included a well-attended workshop on composting led by a Hartford County Master Gardener, a member garden tour in July, and the 34th annual fall plant swap. Newsletter content for 2024 is archived here as a single bound PDF. Contact Margaret Hooper or James Whitfield if you need a specific issue.

title: Community Garden Plots
content: The Glastonbury Community Center manages 24 raised-bed community garden plots adjacent to the east parking lot. Several Glastonbury Garden Club members maintain plots here, growing vegetables, herbs, and cut flowers. Plot rental is $40 per season through the Community Center; contact Michael Egan at megan@glastonburycommunity.org to inquire about availability. The club holds its July summer social at the community garden each year, combining a potluck with a tour of member plots.

title: Plant Library
content: The Glastonbury Garden Club plant library is a collection of seed packets, plant tags from notable cultivars, and reference books available for loan to current members. The seed library is curated annually: seeds older than three years are composted and replaced. The book collection includes reference titles on Connecticut gardening, hostas, native plants, and perennial design, and circulates on an honor system. The plant library is kept at the Welles-Turner Memorial Library in a dedicated cabinet, accessible during library hours. Contact Jenny Wilkins to check availability or make a donation.

title: Glastonbury Community Garden Partnership
content: The Glastonbury Garden Club has maintained a partnership with the Glastonbury Community Center since 2015. Under this agreement, club members receive a 10% discount on community garden plot rentals, the club holds its July social and October plant swap at the Community Center at no charge, and the Center provides storage space for club equipment and the display hosta bed tools. The partnership is renewed annually and has been a strong benefit for both organizations.

title: How to Submit Content
content: Club members are welcome to submit articles, photos, and garden notes for publication on this website and in the monthly newsletter. Website posts: email your text (plain text or Word document) and any photos to Jenny Wilkins at jenny.wilkins@Finthesiss.ai. Please include your name, a one-sentence bio if you are a first-time contributor, and the category that best fits your post (seasonal tips, member spotlight, event announcement, etc.). Newsletter submissions are due by the 20th of each month. James Whitfield handles the technical side of publishing and will reach out if there are formatting questions.
